body_txt: A lot of people love a good grumble. It's cathartic to complain for a bit and get things off their chests. Problem is, "polite society" has a low tolerance level for this kind of thing, and someone who verbalizes their real thoughts on a lousy situation is likely to be frowned upon and labeled a whiner / grouch relatively quickly. For example, the "correct" answer to the question "How are you?" is either "Fine, thank you," or "Pretty good. And you?" (Or, formally, "How are you?") note Though, technically, the correct answer should immediately reveal your status as an atheist or deist. Even if your house exploded in a freak accident that morning and you were flattened by a runaway horse and then fired for coming into work covered in hoofprints. So when people come across a situation where resentment can be voiced, it's not all that surprising when they take full advantage of it. Whether it's online, in print, or among close friends and family, there's always a sense of relief when you're able to voice that irksome thing that's been bugging you for the past week. However, sometimes this can be taken to an extreme. Anyone can get so carried away with the freedom of voicing the things that annoy them that they completely forget to even mention the things that they actively enjoy. It's not that the person is setting out to be a Jerkass or as negative as possible — it's just that they take the good things in life so much for granted that they don't see fit to talk about them... and come across as unbearably cynical as a result. As society becomes more and more cynical (although, in face-to-face interaction, general grouching is still pretty unacceptable, which is why grouching there is different .) this meta-trope is becoming common across the board, manifesting itself in a number of different ways: Often, if an audience, broadcaster, or forum member is challenged about their complaints (e.g. "Do you even like this show?") they'll be genuinely surprised and offended. Of course, they like it! Why would they bother commenting at all on it if they didn't? It's just that grousing about Development Hell , Creator Breakdown , or Fanon Discontinuity is much more fun than counting your blessings — after all, you'd only be preaching to the choir... but then, sometimes you're doing that anyway, if the fanbase is particularly disillusioned enough: the unanimous complaining can often get so toxic and negative that it genuinely seems like the "fanbase" is more like the work's biggest " hatedom ". Some people also point those flaws out for the creator to improve; however, it's far more common for someone to actually be posting destructive criticism than actually trying to be constructive. Given that long-winded essays that are pointing out what the creator(s) of a work did right are mixed in with authorial bias, highly politicized destructive criticism , and flat-out stupidity , it's very easy for what constructive points there are to be blocked out. As a result, accentuating the negative is close to becoming a Discredited Trope (or, at best, an Undead Horse Trope ), as people become just as sick of non-stop unfiltered cynicism and disingenious complaints about the media they like. Often done by the Sour Supporter , a cynical member of a team of goodies; and the Caustic Critic , abrasive, scathing criticism, for your pleasure. Compare Selective Stupidity , doing some Manipulative Editing to make people appear dumber; and He Panned It, Now He Sucks! , a fan reaction when the Accentuate The Negative reviewer pans a show his fans like. See also Sturgeon's Law , Reviewer Stock Phrases , Let's Mock the Monsters , Unpleasable Fanbase .
